steps.title: Identify the key contrast between the two texts
steps.content: Text 1 argues that standardized film and television credits are valuable because they let researchers trace contributions and careers. Text 2 argues that video games lack this consistent crediting, making it hard to trace contributors and answer questions about the medium's development. The author of Text 1 would respond by pointing to the solution that already exists in their medium.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'By recommending that the scholars mentioned in Text 2 consider employing the methods regularly used by film and television researchers': Text 1 describes what film and television themselves do (list credits), not a research method scholars apply. The author makes no recommendation to anyone. ✗
  • Choice B — 'By pointing out that credits have a different intended purpose in film and television than in the medium addressed by the scholars mentioned in Text 2': Credits serve the same purpose in both mediums — identifying contributors. Text 1 does not suggest the purposes differ. ✗
  • Choice C — 'By suggesting that the scholars mentioned in Text 2 rely more heavily on credits as a source of information than film and television researchers do': Video games do not consistently use credits at all, so their scholars cannot rely on them more heavily. This contradicts Text 2. ✗
  • Choice D — 'By observing that a widespread practice in film and television largely prevents the kind of problem faced by the scholars mentioned in Text 2': Text 1 establishes that standardized credits in film and television create a reliable record — exactly the thing Text 2 says is missing from video games. The author of Text 1 would note that the film/television practice prevents the problem Text 2 describes. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
